Fnrn Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs CouprrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r gaslnrnento, CA 9igl4_232g
(9t6) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322_0886
December 6,ZOl0
Sue Mitchell
District Clerk
Tmckee-Donner Recreation and park District
8924 Donner Pass Road
Truckee, California 96161
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-192
Dear Ms. Mitchell:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of peter werbel, a
member of the Board of Directors for the Truckee-Donner Recreation and park District,
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the political Reform Act (the ,.A;;;"i'-"'
Please note this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair political practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as afinder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 71.)
In addition, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, oi other conflicrof-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest.
QUESTTONS
1' May Board Member Werbel participate in decisions related to a potential bond
measure when he owns real property within 500 feet of a property that is the subject of
the govemmental decision?
' The PoliticalReform Act is contained in Government code Sections g1000 through gl0l4. All
statutory ret'erences are to the Government code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Rrlitical Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 t l0 througr, I sssz of ritle 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations' All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division-6 of the Califbrnia Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-t0-192
Page No. 2
2' If Board Member Werbel is disqualified from participating in decisions
involving the potential bond measure, rnoy ir. participate in trre public comment period
like other members of the public and expiess his st piort or opposition to the potential
bond measure?
CONCLUSIONS
l' No' Board Member werbel may not participate in decisions related to the
bond measure because he owns real property within 5ob feet of the property that is the
subject of the governmental decision ord i, presumed to have a disqualifying conflict of
interest' However, there may be certain dec-isions in which he can participate if they are
not inextricably interrelated to the decisions in which he has a conflict. (see discussion
below.)
2. Board Member werbel may remain in a public meeting, listen to the
discussion, and speak as a member of the public soielyregarding his personal interests as
it relates to the bond measure if he qualifie, und..thJ"ffirion in Regulati on lg7oz.4.
You are the District clerk for the Truckee-Donner Recreation and park District
(the "District") and- are writing on behalf of Peter werbel who is a member of the Board
of Directors (the "Board") foithe District. The Board will be making decisions regarding
placement of a potential bond measure on the ballot in2ol1. The potential measure will
include three distinct c-omponents: a performing arts theater/complex, a swimming pool,
and the continuation of three trails within the town of Truckee. Board Member werbel
owns a commercial storefront property that he rents out to a snowboard shop. This
commercial property is within 500 feet of the District's existing community;enter
building, which, as part of the potential bond measure, would be converted to the
proposed performing arts-theater/complex. You ask whether or not Board Member
werbel would be allowed to- participaie in the Board's discussions involving what items
should be placed in the bond measure and the eventual vote on whether to include the
bond measure on the 20ll ballot. You would also like to know if Board Member werbel
may participate in the public meeting as a member of the public if he is aisquaiirieo rrom
participating in the discussions and vote in his capacity as'a Board Member.
FACTS
AIIALYSIs
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or.her official-position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial inteiest. A public official has a.,financial interest,, in a
governmental decision, within the meaning oi the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section g7103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The


File No. A-10-192
Page No. 3
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflici of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 1g700(bx1NA'l.l
step one: Is Board Member werber a,,pubric officiar?r,
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials.,,
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(l).i a "puulic official', is ..every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency (Section g204g.)
fi f,ffil:er 
of the Board, Board Member werbei is a public ornciar within the meaning
step Two: rs Board Memberwerber making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
A public officia-l "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course oi action, or enters into any contrlactual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation rg7o2.r.)
A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within
the authority of his or her position and witf,out significant intervening substantive review,
the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regul ation lg7O2.Z.)
A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultani of his or her agency. (Regulation 1g702.3.)
Board Member werbel is making, participating in makiig, oi irflr"-ncing a governmental
decision when acting in his role is u boa.A *"*b"..
Regulation 18702.3(a) provides that with regard to a governmental decision
within or before the official's own agency, 9r any agency apfointed uy o, suuiect to the
budgetary control 9f ,1" official's ug"r"y, the ofi'ic;l is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if,ror the purpose of influencing the decision,
the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influenc"] ony member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency.
However' Regulation r8702.4provides an exception to the above mle. It states
that a public official may appear beforl his or her agenJy ur ony other member of the
general public in the course of its prescribed governLenial function in order to represent
himself or herself on matters related solely tJhis or t ", "f.rronal interests.,, Such an
2 Because Board luember werbel's position is not one listed in Section g7200 he is not required
to leave the room or announce his conflict of interest to the audience as listed in Regulation 1g702.5.


File No. A-10-192
Page No.4
appearance, properly made, does not constitute making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision. (Regulation fi762'.4(a)(i) ora rcloz.4(bxl).)
An official's "personal interests" include, but are not limited to, an interest in real
property that is wholl owned by the fficial or members of his or her immediately family.
(Regulation 18702.4(b)(lXA).) If this exception applies, Board Member Werbel must
limit his comments to his personal interests, and should make clear that he is not speaking
in the interest of any other person or group, and that he is not acting in any official
capacity. (Burns Advice Letter, No. 4-06-178, citing to McHughAdvice Letter, No. l-
98-324; Gallagher Advice Letter, No. 1-94-279: and-Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-g7-
rsr.)
Furthermore, Board Member Werbel may not discuss his support or opposition to
a decision in which he has a conflict-of-interest with other district officials, boara
members, employees or consultants outside of any public Board meetings. (simonian
Advice Letter, No. 4-08-096; Burns Advice retter, No. 4-06-17g, citin! uliHugn
Advice Letter, No. I-98-324.)
step Three: what are Board Member werbelrs,.economic interests?,,
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 1g703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds'any
position of management (Section g7103(dI Regulation tszor.tG));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(b); Regularion 1g703.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to theGcision (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 9703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more.w^ithin l2 months prior tolhe decision (Section g7-103(ei;"
Regulation 18706.0:


File No. A-10-192
Page No. 5
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" nrlf lsection g7103;
Regulation 18703.5).
From the facts you submitted, the only economic interest indicated is Board
Member Werbel's economic interest in real property. Because you have not provided
any. facts indicating that any other economiqintlrest -uy be afflcted, or.rr analysis is
limited to Board Member werbel's interest in his real piop.rty., ' - --- ----'r'
Step Four: Is Board Member Werbel's economic interest directly or indirecgy
involved in the decision?
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if uny of the following
apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries 1or itre
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision...
"(2)The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sare, purchase, or rease, or inclusion in or
exclusion frol gy city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official his an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property ...
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use
or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on thl real property
in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the prelimirory plrn, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to aaoptitre
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
I A public official always has an economic.interest in his or personal finances. However, any
financial ef-fect a decision may have on Board Member werbel's real'property is considered an effect on his
real property interests and-would not be analyzed separately under the'.:personal financial etl-ects,, rule.
(Regulation 18705'5(a)') Because you do not include infoimation suggJsting rhis economic interest is
implicated, we do not discuss it further.


File No. A-10-192
Page No. 6
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and rear property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is rocated within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redeveropment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer,.storm drainage or similar facilities, and the ."at prop"rty in
which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services.,,
Therefore, because Board Member werbel's real property is located within 500
feet of the proposed performing arts theater/compt"*, t i, ."al property would be directly
involved in any bond measure decisions regarding the performing arts theater/complex.
Your facts indicate that the proposed bond measure has multiple components,
some of which affect property more than 500 feet from Board Member Werbel,s
property. with respect.to those properties, the Board Member,s propertf r"ouro u,
deemed indirectly involved if decisions_involving thosecomponents can be segmentable
(see discussion below). (Regulation 1g704(a).)
step f ive: what is the appricabre materiarity standard?
Regulation 1s705.z(a)(l) provides the materiality standard for directly involved
real property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption.*u{ be rebutted by proof tlat ii is not reasonabry
foreseeable that the gou"rn*"niai decision wilr have anyfinancial effect
on the real property." (Emphasis added.)
under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known as the "one penny rure.J in order to rebut the
presumption' one would have to establish thai the iecision would not even affect the
property's value by one cent.
Indirectly Involved Real p roperty:
The financial effect of a govemmental decision on indirectly involved real
property is presumed not to be material. However, this presumption may be rebutted by
proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the govemmental decision, its
financial effect, and the narure of the rear pro-perty ii *tirr;;ilil;#;;'i", 
,,
economic interest that make it reasonabtyiorer."aute *raJitre decision will have a
material financial 
"{f,!:l9.r.,he real propity in which the public official has an interest.
(Regulation I 8705.2(bX 1).)
Examples of these specific circumstances incrude matters such as the


File No. A-lO-192
Page No. 7
development potential or use of the official's real property or the character of the
neighborhood, including effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood. ln"guiatio, 1g7b5.l(b)irxa_cl.l
Step Six: Reasonably Foreseeable
once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it iis .,reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the goul.n*"rtal decision. (Regulation
18706(a)') An effect need not be certain to be consldered "reasonably foresLeable,,,but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppco;r.lgg I
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (rn re Thorner, supra,
at 198') Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in'proviaing advice (In
re oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to ihe informed judgment of the public
official.
Directly Involved Real property:
While ultimately the determination of whether the financial effect is material must
be left up to Board Member werbel's informed judgment, the financial effect on Board
Member Werbel's economic. interest in his real p-p'."y i. presumed to be material
because the property is within 500 feet of the p-p"rtv .uu;".t to these governmental
decisions' Accordingly, Board Member_werbel may not rirake, participate in making, or
influence governmental decisions regarding the potential bond measure unless he can
rebut the presumption of materiality.
Indirectly Involved Real p roperty:
The financial effect of a governmental decision on indirectly involved real
property is presumed not to be material. However, this presumption may be rebutted by
proof that the decision o-ff-""rf development potential oiuse or ine offici'al's f.op"ay o.
the character of the neighborhood, including traffic, view, privacy, intensity oiuse, noise
levels' air emissions, or similar items. You-have not provided any facts to address these
types of issues. However, as previously stated, the Cbmmission does not act as a finder
of fact when providing advice (In re Oglesby, supra.). Therefore, the determination of
whether or not it is reasonably foreseeable tirat the applicable maieriatity ,tanaara will be
met with regard to decisions involving the potential bond measure, is necessarily a factual
question that is ultimately for Board Member werbel to decide.


File No. A-10-t9Z
Page No. 8
Segmentation
we have advised that it may be possible to segment certain decisions, so long as
the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" to a d-ecision in which an official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest. The Commission has advised that ,.[u]nder certain
circumstances, a public.official disqualified from one decision may p;i"-ip". in other
related decisions provided that the offi"iul', participation does not affect the decision in
which he or she has a conflict of interest.,, (in re (i*"n (r976)2 Fppc opr. zz.l
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for "segmentation" of a governmental
decision:
*(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the
following conditions apply:
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interretut"a to ifr"
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial l.rt"r"rt;- 
---
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered
first and a final decision is reached by-the ug.n"y *i,hout the airquuiiri.a
official's participation in any way: and
(4) once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been
made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a
reopening of or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the
official was disqualified.
(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated,
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify,
or alter the result of another decision.,,
You have stated that the bond measure will consist of three distinct
components: the performing arts theater/complex, a swimming pool, and the
continuation of three trails. The only component in which Board Member werbel
has a potential conflict of interest is the performing arts theiter/complex.
Therefore, if decisions conceming the pirforming"arts theater/complex are made
by the Board without Board Member *erbel's irivolvement before the decisions
involving the other two components of the project, goorJM..ber werbel would
be able to participate in deciiions concerning tihe swimming poor and trail


File No. A-10-192
Page No. 9
components, so long as participation in those decisions has no affect whatsoever
upon the performing arts theater/complex part of the project. However, the facts
you provided indicate that the decision onihe bond measure concerning the three
projects appears to be a single "inextricably interrelated" decision, therefore it
would^be unlikely that Board Member weibet would ue eiigiute to participate in
his official capacity as a Board Member on the bond measrl a"cisio, "uo.riry
before the Board.
Implementation Dec is ions
Board Member werbel may be able to participate in certain decisions that merely
"implement" decisions that have aiready been made * rort as the following three factors
apply:
(1) The decisions for which the official has a disqualifying financial
interest are segregated and decided first;
(2) The remaining decisions will-not 19.sult in reopening or in any way
affect the decisions from which the official was disqual-ifiqd; ani 
--J
(3) The decisions will not independently have a material financial effect
on the official's economic interests. (warne Advice Letter, No. r_02_05);
Olson Advice Letter, No. 4_00_237.)
Implementation decisions merely implement, or carry out, decisions already
made' (Boga Advice Letter, 4-03-067.)- If a particui* a."irion is an i*pir-"rtutio'
decision, it is possible that Board Member werbel 
"ourJ funicipate in that decision
regardless of a conflict arising from another decision, prolrid.d ihe implem"ntution
decision does not independenily create a conflict of inLrest. (warneAdvice Letter,
supra') Board Member werbel must independently assess each decision that is to come
before him to determine if a decision is merely an implementation decision in order to
participate.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally & Legatly Required participation
Even if an official has a conflict ofinterest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the -or.reii, which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103; Regulation tg7 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the officiairnuy-hor" a disqualifying
conflict of interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception
applies only in certain very specific circumstances where the govemment agency would


File No. A-10-192
Page No. l0
be paralyzed from acting. (section g7 10 r ; Reguration l g70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the ..public generalry,, or the
^fi:Ift*ired 
participation" exceptions are applicabre to Board Member werbel,s
crcumstances, so we will not address them furtlier.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
SB jgl

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

However' Regulation r8702.4provides an exception to the above mle. It states

appearance, properly made, does not constitute making, participating in making, or

An official's "personal interests" include, but are not limited to, an interest in real

rsr.)
Furthermore, Board Member Werbel may not discuss his support or opposition to

step Three: what are Board Member werbelrs,.economic interests?,,
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 1g703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds'any
position of management (Section g7103(dI Regulation tszor.tG));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(b); Regularion 1g703.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to theGcision (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 9703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more.w^ithin l2 months prior tolhe decision (Section g7-103(ei;"
Regulation 18706.0:
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" nrlf lsection g7103;
Regulation 18703.5).
From the facts you submitted, the only economic interest indicated is Board
Member Werbel's economic interest in real property. Because you have not provided
any. facts indicating that any other economiqintlrest -uy be afflcted, or.rr analysis is
limited to Board Member werbel's interest in his real piop.rty., ' - --- ----'r'
Step Four: Is Board Member Werbel's economic interest directly or indirecgy
involved in the decision?
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if uny of the following
apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries 1or itre
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the

"(2)The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,

similar decision affecting the real property ...
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use
or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on thl real property
in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the prelimirory plrn, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to aaoptitre
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to

financial ef-fect a decision may have on Board Member werbel's real'property is considered an effect on his
real property interests and-would not be analyzed separately under the'.:personal financial etl-ects,, rule.
(Regulation 18705'5(a)') Because you do not include infoimation suggJsting rhis economic interest is

rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and rear property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is rocated within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redeveropment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer,.storm drainage or similar facilities, and the ."at prop"rty in
which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services.,,
Therefore, because Board Member werbel's real property is located within 500
feet of the proposed performing arts theater/compt"*, t i, ."al property would be directly
involved in any bond measure decisions regarding the performing arts theater/complex.
Your facts indicate that the proposed bond measure has multiple components,
some of which affect property more than 500 feet from Board Member Werbel,s
property. with respect.to those properties, the Board Member,s propertf r"ouro u,
deemed indirectly involved if decisions_involving thosecomponents can be segmentable
(see discussion below). (Regulation 1g704(a).)
step f ive: what is the appricabre materiarity standard?
Regulation 1s705.z(a)(l) provides the materiality standard for directly involved
real property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption.*u{ be rebutted by proof tlat ii is not reasonabry
foreseeable that the gou"rn*"niai decision wilr have anyfinancial effect
on the real property." (Emphasis added.)
under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known as the "one penny rure.J in order to rebut the
presumption' one would have to establish thai the iecision would not even affect the
property's value by one cent.
Indirectly Involved Real p roperty:
The financial effect of a govemmental decision on indirectly involved real
property is presumed not to be material. However, this presumption may be rebutted by
proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the govemmental decision, its
financial effect, and the narure of the rear pro-perty ii *tirr;;ilil;#;;'i", 
,,
economic interest that make it reasonabtyiorer."aute *raJitre decision will have a
material financial

(Regulation I 8705.2(bX 1).)
Examples of these specific circumstances incrude matters such as the

development potential or use of the official's real property or the character of the
neighborhood, including effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood. ln"guiatio, 1g7b5.l(b)irxa_cl.l
Step Six: Reasonably Foreseeable
once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it iis .,reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the goul.n*"rtal decision. (Regulation
18706(a)') An effect need not be certain to be consldered "reasonably foresLeable,,,but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I Fppco;r.lgg I
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (rn re Thorner, supra,
at 198') Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in'proviaing advice (In
re oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to ihe informed judgment of the public
official.
Directly Involved Real property:
While ultimately the determination of whether the financial effect is material must
be left up to Board Member werbel's informed judgment, the financial effect on Board
Member Werbel's economic. interest in his real p-p'."y i. presumed to be material
because the property is within 500 feet of the p-p"rtv .uu;".t to these governmental
decisions' Accordingly, Board Member_werbel may not rirake, participate in making, or
influence governmental decisions regarding the potential bond measure unless he can
rebut the presumption of materiality.
Indirectly Involved Real p roperty:
The financial effect of a governmental decision on indirectly involved real
property is presumed not to be material. However, this presumption may be rebutted by
proof that the decision o-ff-""rf development potential oiuse or ine offici'al's f.op"ay o.
the character of the neighborhood, including traffic, view, privacy, intensity oiuse, noise

met with regard to decisions involving the potential bond measure, is necessarily a factual

You are the District clerk for the Truckee-Donner Recreation and park District
(the "District") and- are writing on behalf of Peter werbel who is a member of the Board
of Directors (the "Board") foithe District. The Board will be making decisions regarding
placement of a potential bond measure on the ballot in2ol1. The potential measure will
include three distinct c-omponents: a performing arts theater/complex, a swimming pool,
and the continuation of three trails within the town of Truckee. Board Member werbel
owns a commercial storefront property that he rents out to a snowboard shop. This
commercial property is within 500 feet of the District's existing community;enter
building, which, as part of the potential bond measure, would be converted to the
proposed performing arts-theater/complex. You ask whether or not Board Member
werbel would be allowed to- participaie in the Board's discussions involving what items
should be placed in the bond measure and the eventual vote on whether to include the
bond measure on the 20ll ballot. You would also like to know if Board Member werbel
may participate in the public meeting as a member of the public if he is aisquaiirieo rrom
participating in the discussions and vote in his capacity as'a Board Member.

QUESTION: that is ultimately for Board Member werbel to decide.


File No. A-10-t9Z
Segmentation
we have advised that it may be possible to segment certain decisions, so long as
the decisions are not "inextricably interrelated" to a d-ecision in which an official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest. The Commission has advised that ,.[u]nder certain
circumstances, a public.official disqualified from one decision may p;i"-ip". in other
related decisions provided that the offi"iul', participation does not affect the decision in
which he or she has a conflict of interest.,, (in re (i*"n (r976)2 Fppc opr. zz.l
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for "segmentation" of a governmental
decision:
*(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the
following conditions apply:
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interretut"a to ifr"
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial l.rt"r"rt;- 
---
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered
first and a final decision is reached by-the ug.n"y *i,hout the airquuiiri.a
official's participation in any way: and
(4) once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been
made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a
reopening of or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the
official was disqualified.
(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated,
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify,
or alter the result of another decision.,,
You have stated that the bond measure will consist of three distinct
components: the performing arts theater/complex, a swimming pool, and the
continuation of three trails. The only component in which Board Member werbel
has a potential conflict of interest is the performing arts theiter/complex.
Therefore, if decisions conceming the pirforming"arts theater/complex are made
by the Board without Board Member *erbel's irivolvement before the decisions
involving the other two components of the project, goorJM..ber werbel would
be able to participate in deciiions concerning tihe swimming poor and trail
components, so long as participation in those decisions has no affect whatsoever
upon the performing arts theater/complex part of the project. However, the facts
you provided indicate that the decision onihe bond measure concerning the three
projects appears to be a single "inextricably interrelated" decision, therefore it
would^be unlikely that Board Member weibet would ue eiigiute to participate in
his official capacity as a Board Member on the bond measrl a"cisio, "uo.riry
before the Board.
Implementation Dec is ions
Board Member werbel may be able to participate in certain decisions that merely
"implement" decisions that have aiready been made * rort as the following three factors
apply:
(1) The decisions for which the official has a disqualifying financial
interest are segregated and decided first;
(2) The remaining decisions will-not 19.sult in reopening or in any way
affect the decisions from which the official was disqual-ifiqd; ani 
--J
(3) The decisions will not independently have a material financial effect
on the official's economic interests. (warne Advice Letter, No. r_02_05);
Olson Advice Letter, No. 4_00_237.)
Implementation decisions merely implement, or carry out, decisions already
made' (Boga Advice Letter, 4-03-067.)- If a particui* a."irion is an i*pir-"rtutio'
decision, it is possible that Board Member werbel 
"ourJ funicipate in that decision
regardless of a conflict arising from another decision, prolrid.d ihe implem"ntution
decision does not independenily create a conflict of inLrest. (warneAdvice Letter,
supra') Board Member werbel must independently assess each decision that is to come
before him to determine if a decision is merely an implementation decision in order to
participate.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally & Legatly Required participation
Even if an official has a conflict ofinterest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the -or.reii, which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87 103; Regulation tg7 07 (a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the officiairnuy-hor" a disqualifying
conflict of interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception
applies only in certain very specific circumstances where the govemment agency would


Page No. l0
be paralyzed from acting. (section g7 10 r ; Reguration l g70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that the ..public generalry,, or the
^fi:Ift*ired 
participation" exceptions are applicabre to Board Member werbel,s
crcumstances, so we will not address them furtlier.

CONCLUSION: l' No' Board Member werbel may not participate in decisions related to the
bond measure because he owns real property within 5ob feet of the property that is the
subject of the governmental decision ord i, presumed to have a disqualifying conflict of
interest' However, there may be certain dec-isions in which he can participate if they are
not inextricably interrelated to the decisions in which he has a conflict. (see discussion
below.)
2. Board Member werbel may remain in a public meeting, listen to the
